summary: Gruber analyzes why Apple skipped its traditional September music event in 2011, arguing that the iPhone has cannibalized the iPod business to the point where new iPods can no longer sustain a major product launch event. He explains that the iPhone 5 announcement slipped to October likely due to production and software readiness, not strategic choice. He revisits his earlier prediction that Apple would need a major iOS device for the fall beyond just iPods, noting his guess of a second iPad was wrong but his reasoning was sound. The core argument is that Apple's fall event matters because it sets the holiday quarter lineup, and iOS devices are now the centerpiece.
key_points: The iPhone has cannibalized iPod sales to the point where new iPod announcements can no longer sustain a major Apple event
Apple likely wanted to announce iPhone 5 in September but needed more time for hardware production, iOS 5, and iCloud
Apple's fall event is strategically critical because it sets the product lineup for the holiday quarter, their strongest sales period
Apple prefers announcing products when ready rather than on arbitrary calendar dates, which is why they dropped Macworld Expo
Nine months into 2011, Apple had announced only one new iOS device (iPad 2), making the fall event unusually high-stakes
money_quotes: Their whole iPod division is propped up by the Touch, which is really just a phone-less iPhone.
Everyone knows, including Apple, that neither the news media nor the masses can get it up for "new iPods" any more. New iOS devices are what we can get it up for.
Apple needs a big iOS device announcement for the fall.
The company prefers to announce new products simply when they're ready, not when some arbitrary date on the calendar arrives. That's why they stopped doing Macworld Expo keynotes.
They showed this year that they're not afraid to do a software-only WWDC keynote. But they can't move Christmas.
iOS devices have "Christmas gift" written all over them.
Nine months into the year and Apple has announced but a single new iOS device: the iPad 2. Time's up, pencils down.
key_insight: The iPhone's cannibalization of the iPod forced Apple to restructure its fall event around major iOS device launches, because only those can generate the anticipation needed to set up the critical holiday quarter.
